title: Śrī Jīrāpallī Pārśva Stava (The Sixth Smaraṇa)
summary: This is a short hymn of remembrance (smaraṇa) to Pārśvanātha, the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, in his famous form enshrined at Jīrāpallī (Jīrāvalā). It is recited for protection — to dispel fear, disease, evil spirits, and obstacles — by calling the Lord's name and meditating on his sacred mantra in the heart. A new reader can simply hear it as a protective prayer: it praises Pārśva, names the guardian deities who serve him, and promises that remembering him calms every danger.
ritualPlacement: It is the sixth of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa), the fixed set of nine protective stotras recited within the Pākṣika (fortnightly) pratikramaṇa, after the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. The Smaraṇas are protective recitations, not one of the six Āvaśyakas. Because the book defines Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī purely by reference to the Pākṣika (with the word-substitution pakkhī → cāumāsī / saṁvatsarī and a longer kāyotsarga), this same Smaraṇa recurs unchanged in those two pratikramaṇas; it has no independent text of its own there.
authorAndSource: Attributed in this book to Merutuṅgasūri (the verse colophon, vv.11 and 14, names Śrī Merutuṅgasūri), a 14th–15th-century ācārya of the Añcalagaccha (Vidhipakṣa) tradition. The hymn praises Pārśvanātha of Jīrāpallī / Jīrāvalā — a celebrated Pārśva tīrtha in Sirohī, Rājasthān. (Note: some modern sources attach the name 'Jīrāpallī Pārśva stava' to other authors; this preface follows the attribution printed in the source book and flags the variance in notes.)
structureArc: vv.1–2 open with salutation to Pārśvanātha as bestower of all welfare, invoking his mantric (hrīṃ) form and the guardian deities Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī who worship his feet. vv.3–4 deploy the seed-syllable (bīja) mantra itself — the Name that destroys hostile pretas and piśācas (v.3) and the stambhana ('immobilize, immobilize, svāhā') formula that wards off approaching afflictions of karma (v.4). vv.5–7 give the tāntric meditation method: visualizing the mantra rising through the body from the navel-channel to the brahma-randhra upon an eight-petalled lotus (v.5), the four- and five-coloured tattva meditated in sequence (v.6), and the pañcāṅga (five-limbed) protective placement that subdues all fortunes to the meditator (v.7). v.8 states the principle: suffering binds a person only until this 'mantra-king' awakens, embodied, in the heart. v.9 is the classic bhayahara litany — fear from disease, bondage, slaughter, serpents, fire, and water perishes merely by remembering Pārśva's name. v.10 describes yogic absorption, as a yogi becomes one with inner sound (nāda). vv.11–14 form the colophon: naming Merutuṅgasūri (v.11), recalling that Jīrāpallī Pārśva is served by the Pārśva-yakṣa and worshipped by Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī (v.12), declaring the hymn 'made of all mantras' and destroyer of spirits (v.13), and closing with the poet meditating on the Lord in his heart to attain all siddhi (v.14).
recurringImagery: Three motifs run across the hymn and are explained here once. (1) The mantra-king in the heart-lotus (mantrarāja … hṛdi … mūrtimān): the Name/seed-mantra of Pārśva is personified as a sovereign that must 'awaken' embodied within the practitioner's heart; vv.4, 8 and 13–14 all turn on this image, and v.5–7 supply the physical map for it — the body's inner channels from navel (nābhi-nāla) up to the crown-aperture (brahma-randhra), and the eight-petalled lotus (aṣṭa-dala padma) on which the coloured tattva is visualized. (2) The two guardian deities (śāsana-devatā): Dharaṇendra (the nāga-king) and Padmāvatī (the yakṣiṇī), the protective deities specific to Pārśvanātha, appear in vv.2 and 12 framing the praise. (3) The litany of great fears (bhaya): v.9 lists the canonical dangers — disease, bondage, violent death, serpents, fire, water — that the Name dissolves; this is the shared 'bhayahara' (fear-removing) theme that also defines the neighbouring Smaraṇas to Pārśva (the Upasargahara, no.4, and Namiūṇa Bhayahara, no.5).
keyThemes: Seed-mantra (bīja) and the mantra-king meditated in the heart
Tāntric body-meditation: navel-channel, brahma-randhra, eight-petalled lotus
Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī as Pārśva's guardian deities
Removal of the great fears (bhayahara): disease, bondage, death, serpents, fire, water
Destruction of evil spirits (bhūta, preta, piśāca)
Yogic absorption in inner sound (nāda) and attainment of siddhi
Devotion to the Jīrāpallī / Jīrāvalā Pārśva tīrtha

glossary.definition: A 'remembrance' — a short protective stotra recited from memory; this is the sixth of the nine such Smaraṇas in the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
A hymn of praise to a Tīrthaṅkara or deity.
'King of mantras' — the supreme seed-mantra of Pārśva that the verse says must awaken, embodied, in the practitioner's heart.
A 'seed' syllable (e.g. hrīṃ, oṃ, kṣipa, svāhā) that concentrates a mantra's power; several appear verbatim in vv.3–4, 7.
A tāntric act of 'immobilizing' or arresting — here halting approaching karmic afflictions ('stambhaya stambhaya svāhā', v.4).
The supernatural excellences or marks of perfection of a Tīrthaṅkara (v.2 calls Pārśva endowed with crores of pure atiśayas).
The serpent-king (nāgendra) who is the male guardian deity (yakṣa) of Pārśvanātha.
The yakṣiṇī (female guardian deity) of Pārśvanātha, worshipped alongside Dharaṇendra.
The 'aperture of brahman' at the crown of the head, the upper point of the inner meditation-channel (v.5).
The 'eight-petalled lotus' visualized in the body as the seat of the meditated tattva (v.5).
'Five limbs' — the five-fold protective placement (nyāsa) of mantra-syllables on the body for guarding the meditator (v.7).
Here the 'essence/reality' that is meditated upon — the coloured mantra-form held in the lotus that yields all results (vv.5–7).
The subtle inner sound with which the yogi merges; the verse likens absorption in the mantra to a yogi's union with nāda (v.10).
Accomplishment or attainment — both worldly success and, ultimately, spiritual fulfilment; the hymn closes promising 'all siddhi' (vv.11, 14).
Fear/danger; v.9 lists the canonical great fears that remembering Pārśva's name dissolves.
Classes of malevolent spirits and ghosts that the Name and mantra are said to drive away (vv.3, 13).
The renowned Pārśvanātha pilgrimage site (Jīrāvalā, Sirohī, Rājasthān) after which this form of the Lord is named.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as a protective prayer to Pārśvanātha — praising him, naming his guardian deities, and trusting that remembering his name dispels fear and harm (focus on vv.1–2, 9, 12). An advanced practitioner will recognize it as a tāntric mantra-stotra and follow vv.3–8 as an actual meditation manual — bīja-syllables, the navel-to-crown inner channel, the eight-petalled lotus, the coloured tattva, and the pañcāṅga-nyāsa — culminating in the heart-borne 'mantra-king' and yogic absorption.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Authoritative source text, ritual placement of the Nine Smaraṇas within Pākṣika, and the attribution of this Smaraṇa to Merutuṅgasūri.

sources.title: Encyclopaedia entry: Pārśvanātha and his śāsana-devatās Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Pārśvanātha as the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara and his guardian deities Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī.

sources.title: Jirawala Parshvanath Tirtha (Sirohi, Rajasthan)
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the Jīrāpallī / Jīrāvalā Pārśvanātha pilgrimage site.

notes: Authorship follows the source book, whose colophon (vv.11, 14) names Śrī Merutuṅgasūri, traditionally the Añcalagaccha (Vidhipakṣa) ācārya of the 14th–15th c. Some popular online sources instead attribute a 'Jīrāpallī Pārśva' hymn to other authors and conflate it with the Bhayahara/Upasargahara stotras; this divergence is unresolved and the book is treated as the golden source here. The bīja-syllable strings in vv.3–4, 7 are mantra-text and should be preserved verbatim, not 'translated'. The reading 'Jirikāpallī' (v.11) versus 'Jirāpallī' (v.12) is the book's own variation and is kept as printed. The precise enumeration of the great fears in v.9 (whether six, seven, or eight) is read directly from the verse and should be confirmed against the per-verse text.
